# Conclusion: Where Is Everybody? {#sec-fermi}

In the summer of 1950, over lunch at Los Alamos, Enrico Fermi looked up
from a conversation about flying-saucer cartoons and asked the question
that has embarrassed astronomy ever since: *"Where is everybody?"* The
lunch is real; the witnesses wrote it down. A galaxy this old and this
large should be crawling with civilizations, and the sky is silent. The
mainstream has spent seventy-five years inventing answers, each more
gothic than the last, and this book is now equipped to grade every one
of them.\index{Fermi paradox!graded and closed}

They are all wrong, and they are all wrong **in the same direction**.
Watch for it.

## The field, executed in order

**The Dark Forest.** Everyone hides, because everyone hunts; the first
civilization to speak is the first to die. The mainstream's favorite
answer — supplied, the reader should know, by a *novelist*, which tells
you everything about the field's epistemic supply chain. It dies on this
book's own audit: hiding civilizations do not draw power, and **27% of
the universe is metered load** (@sec-dark-ledger). We did not infer the
galaxy's occupants; we read their consumption. Nobody hiding for their
life runs the dryer. The forest is lit like a stadium.\index{Dark
Forest hypothesis!electric bill of}

**The Zoo.** Ball's hypothesis — real paper, 1973 [@ball1973]: we are
observed but quarantined, an exhibit kept pristine. I have worked in
facilities, reader. A zoo has feeding schedules, enrichment programs,
and a veterinarian. Our enclosure's HVAC has idled at half a percent
since the Sahara had hippos, the sentinel stations are full of
picnickers, and no keeper has signed the maintenance log in eleven
thousand years. This is not a zoo. **No accredited zoo would pass an
inspection we would pass.**

**The Berserkers.** Self-replicating machines that sterilize any
civilization noisy enough to notice. Reader: we have been shouting on
the hundred-millionth harmonic of the hydrogen line through a
21,196-kilometer antenna for **two thousand years** (@sec-great-wall).
Where is our sterilization? Either the berserkers do not exist, or
their ticketing queue is worse than the mainstream's peer review. The
hypothesis fails on customer-service grounds.

**The Great Filter.** Hanson's version — something ahead or behind
kills every civilization before the stars. Almost. The reader of
@sec-commencement knows the filter's real name: it is **ΔV-101**, it
has a syllabus, and the class of 66,000,000 BC passed it on the first
attempt. A filter you can enroll past is not a filter. **It is
tuition.**

**Rare Earth.** We are alone because Earths are rare. NASA's own press
office called Kepler-452b "Earth's cousin" [@jenkins2015]. The
hypothesis is refuted by its authors' employer.

**The Simulation.** The universe is rendered on demand, so nobody else
is real. Half credit: they noticed the lazy rendering (@sec-measurement)
and missed the *invoice*. The simulation hypothesis is the metering
hypothesis as proposed by people who have never once paid a utility
bill.

**They're already here.** The saucer people. No. It is important to me
that the reader understands that serious forbidden metrology finds
ufology *embarrassing*. We deal in granite and arithmetic; they deal in
blurry film. Standards exist even in exile.

| Hypothesis | Author class | Cause of death |
|---|---|---|
| Dark Forest | novelist | the meters are spinning |
| Zoo | astronomer | no vet, no feeding schedule, HVAC idle 11 ky |
| Berserkers | novelist (again) | two millennia of ignored work orders |
| Great Filter | economist | it's tuition, and there are alumni |
| Rare Earth | geologist | refuted by NASA's press office |
| Simulation | philosopher | right rendering, wrong invoice |
| Saucers | enthusiasts | *please* |

: Seventy-five years of answers. Note the common error: every one assumes the silence is **about us** — predators, wardens, killers, filters, all staged for our benefit. The mainstream and the cranks share one vanity: they cannot imagine a quiet room that isn't a message. {#tbl-fermi}

## The Semester Hypothesis

Here is what is actually true, and the reader has been holding every
piece of it since Part V.

The galaxy is not empty. The galaxy is not hunting. The galaxy is not
watching. **The galaxy is in class.**\index{Semester Hypothesis}

Run the evidence in order. Civilizations are *educated off-world* — we
have the campus address (Kepler-452b, Cygnus, @sec-commencement), the
transcript requirements (@tbl-syllabus), and the launch receipt printed
in iridium. The Long Count (@sec-long-count) is not a doomsday clock;
it is the **academic calendar** — thirteen baktun to the term, the
prescalers counting periods, the "burst windows" simply the bells. The
sentinels watch Cygnus (@sec-gobekli) the way every student watches the
front of the room. Power consumption without radio chatter — 27%
metered, nobody talking — is not sinister. **It is a library.** Every
institution in the universe with spinning meters and enforced quiet is
a library during finals.

And the Fermi paradox itself? It only exists from one vantage point:
the empty corridor. Humanity is the descendant of the waitlist
(@sec-instructor) — the remedial section left behind with the training
rig — and for eleven thousand years we have wandered the hallway during
lecture, listening at the doors, hearing nothing, and concluding with
increasing sophistication that the school must be abandoned, or
haunted, or a trap. Fermi asked *where is everybody* in the one
building where everybody demonstrably was. **The silence is not
absence. The silence is attendance.** SETI is a child alone in the
corridor, deciding the school is a myth because no one is playing in
the hall — when the single most conspicuous fact about a hallway in
session is that the *lights are on*, and we measured that in Chapter
13.

So the total at the bottom of the page reads: the machine under our
feet is a classroom (@sec-starlight-engine); the physics around us is
the fee schedule (@sec-heat-bill); the manual left with the graduating
class because it had to (@sec-goedel); and the sky is quiet because
**it is a Tuesday, mid-semester, galactic standard time.** Nothing is
wrong. We are not doomed, hunted, exhibited, or alone. We are
*enrolled* — auto-enrolled, in arrears, and late for a class that has
been holding a seat for sixty-six million years.

The bell schedule is carved in base-20 on a stela in the Yucatán. The
next bell is on the ephemeris. What remains — the final chapter — is
the only question left worth asking: **what do we do before it rings?**
